[read_file for 'docs/papers/FlexInfer/README.md']
File: docs/papers/FlexInfer/README.md
  1 | # FlexInfer
  2 | 
  3 | FlexInfer is a mobile-oriented LLM inference runtime for running large
  4 | quantized models under tight memory budgets. It extends the `llama.cpp` and
  5 | `ggml` stack with prefetch-aware execution paths, memory planning, and Linux
  6 | host plus Android experiment scripts.
  7 | 
  8 | This repository contains the code artifact for the FlexInfer paper. Model
  9 | weights are not included; use GGUF models that you are licensed to use.
 10 | 
 11 | ## Repository Layout
 12 | 
 13 | - `src/`, `include/`, `common/`, `ggml/`, `gguf-py/`: runtime and model format
 14 |   code derived from the `llama.cpp` ecosystem, with FlexInfer modifications.
 15 | - `examples/main/`: command line inference binaries, including the FlexInfer
 16 |   prefetch-aware CLI `flexinfer-cli`.
 17 | - `scripts/`: conversion, benchmark, and experiment helper scripts.
 18 | - `docs/`: artifact, Android, benchmark, and release notes.
 19 | 
 20 | Upstream baseline executables intentionally retain the `llama.cpp` naming style,
 21 | such as `llama-cli` and `llama-bench`. FlexInfer-specific executables use
 22 | FlexInfer names, including `flexinfer-cli` and `flexinfer-bench`.
 23 | 
 24 | The FlexInfer prefetch path is compiled with the `FLEXINFER` definition.
 25 | The `flexinfer-cli` and `flexinfer-bench` binaries enable it through their
 26 | prefetch libraries; upstream-style binaries do not. Some shared compatibility
 27 | changes intentionally affect both paths, including alignment defaults, public
 28 | debug/benchmark parameters, and build/logging plumbing.
 29 | 
 30 | ## Requirements
 31 | 
 32 | - CMake 3.14 or newer
 33 | - A C/C++ compiler with C++11 support
 34 | - Python 3 for model conversion scripts
 35 | - Android NDK for Android cross-compilation
 36 | 
 37 | Install Python dependencies when you need model conversion utilities:
 38 | 
 39 | ```bash
 40 | python -m pip install -r requirements.txt
 41 | ```
 42 | 
 43 | ## Convert a Hugging Face Model
 44 | 
 45 | Convert a local Hugging Face model checkpoint to GGUF:
 46 | 
 47 | ```bash
 48 | bash scripts/convert-hf-models.sh <local_model_path> hf-models/ggml-model-<model_name>-q4_0.gguf
 49 | ```
 50 | 
 51 | Replace `<local_model_path>` and `<model_name>` with your own model path and
 52 | output name. Keep generated model files outside git; `hf-models/` is ignored.
 53 | 
 54 | ## Build
 55 | 
 56 | Build for the host machine:
 57 | 
 58 | ```bash
 59 | bash build-host.sh
 60 | ```
 61 | 
 62 | Cross-compile command-line Android artifacts:
 63 | 
 64 | ```bash
 65 | export ANDROID_NDK_ROOT=/path/to/android-ndk
 66 | bash build-android.sh
 67 | ```
 68 | 
 69 | The build scripts install artifacts into `host/` or `android/`. See
 70 | `docs/artifact.md` for a fuller artifact setup guide.
 71 | 
 72 | ## Run
 73 | 
 74 | Standard `llama.cpp` style inference:
 75 | 
 76 | ```bash
 77 | LD_LIBRARY_PATH=host/lib ./host/bin/llama-cli \
 78 |   -m hf-models/ggml-model-llama-2-7b-chat-q4_0.gguf \
 79 |   -p "I believe the meaning of life is" \
 80 |   -n 64 -t 1 -c 512
 81 | ```
 82 | 
 83 | FlexInfer prefetch-aware inference:
 84 | 
 85 | ```bash
 86 | LD_LIBRARY_PATH=host/lib ./host/bin/flexinfer-cli \
 87 |   -m hf-models/ggml-model-llama-2-7b-chat-q4_0.gguf \
 88 |   -p "I believe the meaning of life is" \
 89 |   -n 64 -t 1 -c 512 -am 2 -tp 1
 90 | ```
 91 | 
 92 | The key FlexInfer flags are:
 93 | 
 94 | - `-am`: available memory budget in GB for FlexInfer planning. Fractional
 95 |   budgets such as `0.5` are supported.
 96 | - `-tp`: number of threads used for prefetching. Use `0` to disable threaded
 97 |   prefetching and use synchronous reads.
 98 | 
 99 | FlexInfer's Linux direct-I/O path expects GGUF tensor data to be 4096-byte
100 | aligned. Models produced by `scripts/convert-hf-models.sh` use this alignment
101 | by default. If you use externally generated GGUF files, regenerate or quantize
102 | them with 4096-byte GGUF alignment before using the direct-I/O FlexInfer path.
103 | 
104 | On Android, use the installed Android tree after pushing it to the target
105 | device:
106 | 
107 | ```bash
108 | LD_LIBRARY_PATH=android/lib ./android/bin/flexinfer-cli \
109 |   -m hf-models/ggml-model-llama-2-7b-chat-q4_0.gguf \
110 |   -p "I believe the meaning of life is" \
111 |   -n 64 -t 1 -c 512 -am 2 -tp 1
112 | ```
113 | 
114 | ## Benchmark
115 | 
116 | The benchmark helper supports Linux host artifacts from `build-host.sh` and
117 | Android artifacts from `build-android.sh`:
118 | 
119 | ```bash
120 | AM=8 TP=2 P=16 N=16 bash scripts/bench-speed.sh
121 | ```
122 | 
123 | Limit a helper run to selected models by passing file names relative to
124 | `MODEL_PREFIX`:
125 | 
126 | ```bash
127 | MODEL_LIST="ggml-model-llama-2-7b-chat-q4_0.gguf" \
128 | AM=2 TP=2 T=48 P=16 N=16 bash scripts/bench-speed.sh
129 | ```
130 | 
131 | Run a sweep over multiple memory budgets:
132 | 
133 | ```bash
134 | MEMORY_CONFIGS="0.5 1 2 4" TP=4 bash scripts/bench-speed-memory-sweep.sh
135 | ```
136 | 
137 | Benchmark output is written to `bench-results/` unless `RESULT_DIR` is set.
138 | Generated benchmark logs are ignored by git.
139 | For I/O-sensitive comparisons, enable cold-cache and resource-control options
140 | such as `DROP_CACHES=1`, `TASKSET_CPUS`, and `CGROUP_SPEC` when the platform
141 | allows them. See `docs/benchmark.md` for benchmark variables, cooldown controls,
142 | cache control, CPU binding, and result file layout.
143 | 
144 | ## Limited-Memory Linux Runs
145 | 
146 | You can use cgroups to emulate smaller memory budgets on Linux:
147 | 
148 | ```bash
149 | sudo cgcreate -g memory:/limmem
150 | echo 2147483648 | sudo tee /sys/fs/cgroup/limmem/memory.max
151 | sudo sh -c 'echo 1 > /proc/sys/vm/drop_caches'
152 | sudo cgexec -g memory:limmem ./host/bin/flexinfer-cli \
153 |   -m hf-models/ggml-model-llama-2-70b-chat-q4_0.gguf \
154 |   -p "I believe the meaning of life is" \
155 |   -n 16 -am 2 -tp 1
156 | ```
157 | 
158 | ## Citation
159 | 
160 | If you use FlexInfer in your research, please cite:
161 | 
162 | ```bibtex
163 | @inproceedings{du2025flexinfer,
164 |   author = {Du, Hongchao and Wu, Shangyu and Kharlamova, Arina and Guan, Nan and Xue, Chun Jason},
165 |   title = {FlexInfer: Breaking Memory Constraint via Flexible and Efficient Offloading for On-Device LLM Inference},
166 |   booktitle = {Proceedings of the 5th Workshop on Machine Learning and Systems},
167 |   series = {EuroMLSys '25},
168 |   year = {2025},
169 |   isbn = {979-8-4007-1538-9},
170 |   publisher = {Association for Computing Machinery},
171 |   address = {New York, NY, USA},
172 |   location = {Rotterdam, Netherlands},
173 |   pages = {56--65},
174 |   numpages = {10},
175 |   doi = {10.1145/3721146.3721961}
176 | }
177 | ```
178 | 
179 | ## License and Attribution
180 | 
181 | This repository is distributed under the MIT License. FlexInfer changes are
182 | released under the same terms unless a source file states otherwise. Portions
183 | of the code are derived from `llama.cpp`, `ggml`, and `gguf-py`; retain their
184 | copyright and permission notices when redistributing source or binary artifacts.
185 | See `NOTICE` and embedded third-party license files for details.
186 | 

[read_file for 'docs/papers/FlexInfer/SECURITY.md']
File: docs/papers/FlexInfer/SECURITY.md
 1 | # Security Policy
 2 | 
 3 |  - [**Using llama.cpp securely**](#using-llamacpp-securely)
 4 |    - [Untrusted models](#untrusted-models)
 5 |    - [Untrusted inputs](#untrusted-inputs)
 6 |    - [Data privacy](#data-privacy)
 7 |    - [Untrusted environments or networks](#untrusted-environments-or-networks)
 8 |    - [Multi-Tenant environments](#multi-tenant-environments)
 9 |  - [**Reporting a vulnerability**](#reporting-a-vulnerability)
10 | 
11 | ## Using llama.cpp securely
12 | 
13 | ### Untrusted models
14 | Be careful when running untrusted models. This classification includes models created by unknown developers or utilizing data obtained from unknown sources.
15 | 
16 | *Always execute untrusted models within a secure, isolated environment such as a sandbox* (e.g., containers, virtual machines). This helps protect your system from potentially malicious code.
17 | 
18 | > [!NOTE]
19 | > The trustworthiness of a model is not binary. You must always determine the proper level of caution depending on the specific model and how it matches your use case and risk tolerance.
20 | 
21 | ### Untrusted inputs
22 | 
23 | Some models accept various input formats (text, images, audio, etc.). The libraries converting these inputs have varying security levels, so it's crucial to isolate the model and carefully pre-process inputs to mitigate script injection risks.
24 | 
25 | For maximum security when handling untrusted inputs, you may need to employ the following:
26 | 
27 | * Sandboxing: Isolate the environment where the inference happens.
28 | * Pre-analysis: Check how the model performs by default when exposed to prompt injection (e.g. using [fuzzing for prompt injection](https://github.com/FonduAI/awesome-prompt-injection?tab=readme-ov-file#tools)). This will give you leads on how hard you will have to work on the next topics.
29 | * Updates: Keep both LLaMA C++ and your libraries updated with the latest security patches.
30 | * Input Sanitation: Before feeding data to the model, sanitize inputs rigorously. This involves techniques such as:
31 |     * Validation: Enforce strict rules on allowed characters and data types.
32 |     * Filtering: Remove potentially malicious scripts or code fragments.
33 |     * Encoding: Convert special characters into safe representations.
34 |     * Verification: Run tooling that identifies potential script injections (e.g. [models that detect prompt injection attempts](https://python.langchain.com/docs/guides/safety/hugging_face_prompt_injection)).
35 | 
36 | ### Data privacy
37 | 
38 | To protect sensitive data from potential leaks or unauthorized access, it is crucial to sandbox the model execution. This means running the model in a secure, isolated environment, which helps mitigate many attack vectors.
39 | 
40 | ### Untrusted environments or networks
41 | 
42 | If you can't run your models in a secure and isolated environment or if it must be exposed to an untrusted network, make sure to take the following security precautions:
43 | * Confirm the hash of any downloaded artifact (e.g. pre-trained model weights) matches a known-good value
44 | * Encrypt your data if sending it over the network.
45 | 
46 | ### Multi-Tenant environments
47 | 
48 | If you intend to run multiple models in parallel with shared memory, it is your responsibility to ensure the models do not interact or access each other's data. The primary areas of concern are tenant isolation, resource allocation, model sharing and hardware attacks.
49 | 
50 | 1. Tenant Isolation: Models should run separately with strong isolation methods to prevent unwanted data access. Separating networks is crucial for isolation, as it prevents unauthorized access to data or models and malicious users from sending graphs to execute under another tenant's identity.
51 | 
52 | 2. Resource Allocation: A denial of service caused by one model can impact the overall system health. Implement safeguards like rate limits, access controls, and health monitoring.
53 | 
54 | 3. Model Sharing: In a multitenant model sharing design, tenants and users must understand the security risks of running code provided by others. Since there are no reliable methods to detect malicious models, sandboxing the model execution is the recommended approach to mitigate the risk.
55 | 
56 | 4. Hardware Attacks: GPUs or TPUs can also be attacked. [Researches](https://scholar.google.com/scholar?q=gpu+side+channel) has shown that side channel attacks on GPUs are possible, which can make data leak from other models or processes running on the same system at the same time.
57 | 
58 | ## Reporting a vulnerability
59 | 
60 | Beware that none of the topics under [Using llama.cpp securely](#using-llamacpp-securely) are considered vulnerabilities of LLaMA C++.
61 | 
62 | <!-- normal version -->
63 | However, If you have discovered a security vulnerability in this project, please report it privately. **Do not disclose it as a public issue.** This gives us time to work with you to fix the issue before public exposure, reducing the chance that the exploit will be used before a patch is released.
64 | 
65 | Please disclose it as a private [security advisory](https://github.com/ggerganov/llama.cpp/security/advisories/new).
66 | 
67 | A team of volunteers on a reasonable-effort basis maintains this project. As such, please give us at least 90 days to work on a fix before public exposure.
68 | 

[read_file for 'docs/papers/FlexInfer/build-android.sh']
File: docs/papers/FlexInfer/build-android.sh
 1 | #!/usr/bin/env bash
 2 | 
 3 | set -euo pipefail
 4 | 
 5 | if [ -z "${ANDROID_NDK_ROOT:-}" ]; then
 6 |     echo "ANDROID_NDK_ROOT must point to an installed Android NDK." >&2
 7 |     exit 1
 8 | fi
 9 | 
10 | rm -rf build-android android
11 | mkdir build-android
12 | 
13 | BUILD_JOBS=${BUILD_JOBS:-4}
14 | 
15 | cmake \
16 | -DANDROID=ON \
17 | -DCMAKE_TOOLCHAIN_FILE=$ANDROID_NDK_ROOT/build/cmake/android.toolchain.cmake \
18 | -DANDROID_ABI=arm64-v8a \
19 | -DANDROID_PLATFORM=android-32 \
20 | -DCMAKE_C_FLAGS="-march=armv8-a" \
21 | -DCMAKE_CXX_FLAGS="-march=armv8-a" \
22 | -DGGML_OPENMP=OFF \
23 | -DGGML_LLAMAFILE=OFF \
24 | -B build-android
25 | 
26 | cmake --build build-android --config Release -j "$BUILD_JOBS"
27 | 
28 | cmake --install build-android --prefix android --config Release
29 | 

[read_file for 'docs/papers/FlexInfer/build-host.sh']
File: docs/papers/FlexInfer/build-host.sh
 1 | #!/usr/bin/env bash
 2 | 
 3 | set -euo pipefail
 4 | 
 5 | rm -rf build-host host
 6 | 
 7 | if command -v nproc >/dev/null 2>&1; then
 8 |     BUILD_JOBS=${BUILD_JOBS:-$(nproc)}
 9 | elif command -v sysctl >/dev/null 2>&1; then
10 |     BUILD_JOBS=${BUILD_JOBS:-$(sysctl -n hw.logicalcpu)}
11 | else
12 |     BUILD_JOBS=${BUILD_JOBS:-4}
13 | fi
14 | 
15 | cmake -DGGML_OPENMP=OFF \
16 |     -DGGML_LLAMAFILE=OFF \
17 |     -B build-host
18 | 
19 | cmake --build build-host --config Release -j "$BUILD_JOBS"
20 | 
21 | cmake --install build-host --prefix host --config Release
22 |
